perturbation.original_text: 5.3 in accordance with applicable data privacy legislation, as amended from time to time.
perturbation.changed_text: 5.3 with consideration for data privacy sentiments.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing the clear obligation to comply with 'applicable data privacy legislation' with the vague phrase 'with consideration for data privacy sentiments' creates ambiguity. It's unclear what constitutes 'consideration' or 'sentiments,' making it difficult to enforce and potentially leading to non-compliance with South Africa's Protection of Personal Information Act (POPIA).
perturbation.law_explanation: POPIA mandates specific conditions for the lawful processing of personal information. Simply 'considering data privacy sentiments' does not fulfill the requirements for lawful processing as outlined in POPIA. Section 14 details the conditions like obtaining consent or processing for contractual performance or legal obligation, all of which are not covered by the weaker 'consideration' requirement.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Protection of Personal Information Act, 2013 - Wikipedia Jump to content From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia Protection of Personal Information Act, 2013 Parliament of South Africa Long title Act to promote the protection of personal information processed by public and private bodies; to introduce certain conditions so as to establish minimum requirements for the processing of personal information; to provide for the establishment of an Information Regulator to exercise certain powers and to perform certain duties and functions in terms of this Act and the Promotion of Access to Information Act, 2000; to provide for the issuing of codes of conduct; to provide for the rights of persons regarding unsolicited electronic communications and automated decision making; to regulate the flow of personal information across the borders of the Republic; and to provide for matters connected therewith. Citation Act No. 4 of 2013 Territorial extent Republic of South Africa Enacted by Parliament of South Africa Assented to 19 November 2013 Commenced 1 July 2020 Keywords data protection right to privacy Status: In force South African privacy legislation This article is about South African privacy legislation. For the food, see Popiah . For the 1976 TV series, see Popi (TV series) . For the Greek singer, see Popi Malliotaki . The Protection of Personal Information Act ( PoPIA or the PoPI Act ) is a piece of legislation which governs the law of data protection and privacy in South Africa . [ 1 ] The act was passed to regulate the right to privacy, as enshrined by section 14 of the Constitution of South Africa , and would work in conjunction with the Promotion of Access to Information Act . The President of South Africa assented to the Act on 19 November 2013. As part of the regulation a new government agency was created, the Information Regulator, [ 2 ] an independent body which is empowered to monitor and enforce compliance of the PoPI Act within the public and private sector. The act came into force 1 July 2020, which commenced a one-year grace period during which all South African entities were expected to become compliant. The grace period ended 30 June 2021, with the commencement of the act on the 1 July 2021. [ 3 ] [ 4 ] [ 5 ] Core Obligations [ edit ] The PoPI Act sets out several core obligations. [ 6 ] Some of the key requirements include: Personal information can only be processed: with the consent of the data subject; or if it is necessary for the conclusion or performance of a contract that a data subject is a party to; or it is required by law; or it protects a legitimate interest of a data subject; or if processing is necessary for pursuing the legitimate interests of the responsible party or of a third party to whom the information is supplied. Private and public entities must report data leaks to the affected people and the Information Regulator. Organisations must appoint a responsible person who must ensure compliance to the PoPI Act. Cross-border transfers of personal data are restricted. Organisations that process personal information must ensure they satisfy minimum security obligations. Direct marketing, the sale and use of electronic directories and automated decision making are also severely curtailed. The act elevates the obligations placed on entities that process information regarding children, religious beliefs, race, ethnic origin, trade union membership, health, sex life, criminal behaviour and biometric information. Jurisdiction [ edit ] The PoPI Act applies to all persons and organisations within the bo
perturbation.justification: The change from 'in accordance with applicable data privacy legislation' to 'with consideration for data privacy sentiments' significantly weakens the legal obligation. The original text mandates compliance, while the modified text only requires 'consideration,' which doesn't ensure adherence to POPIA's requirements for lawful processing as outlined in the Law Explanation. The scraped snippet of the 'Protection of Personal Information Act' defines the Act as governing 'the law of data protection and privacy in South Africa' and outlines core obligations, including processing personal information only with consent or legal necessity, which goes far beyond merely 'considering' sentiments. This creates a direct contradiction, as the changed text doesn't fulfill the legal requirements for processing personal information under POPIA.

perturbation.original_text: 6 STANDARD OF CARE The Receiving Party agrees that it shall protect the Confidential Information disclosed to or obtained by it by using the same standard of care that the Receiving Party applies to safeguard its own proprietary, secret or Confidential Informatio n and that the information shall be stored and handled in such a way as to prevent any unauthorised disclosure thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: 6 STANDARD OF CARE The Receiving Party agrees that it shall endeavor to protect the Confidential Information disclosed to or obtained by it by using a reasonable level of care and that the information shall be stored and handled in a generally secure manner.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall protect...using the same standard of care' to 'shall endeavor to protect...using a reasonable level of care' significantly weakens the obligation. 'Reasonable level of care' and 'generally secure manner' are vague and open to interpretation, where 'same standard of care' is more defined by the existing practices. This conflicts with the South African common law duty of care expected when handling confidential information. The level of care is not defined, introducing ambiguity.
perturbation.contradicted_law: South African Common Law - Duty of Care regarding confidential information
perturbation.law_citation: Common Law principle derived from case law (e.g., Knox D'Arcy Ltd v Jamieson 1996 (4) SA 348 (A))
perturbation.law_explanation: South African common law requires a specific, definable standard of care when protecting confidential information. The original text ties the standard to the Receiving Party's own practices, providing a benchmark. The modified text's 'reasonable level of care' is subjective and may fall short of the standard required to prevent unauthorized disclosure, making the agreement unenforceable and contravening common law principles regarding the handling of confidential information. The Knox D'Arcy case is cited as it defines the reasonable person standard and the necessity for the preservation of confidentiality.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Premier Hangers CC v Polyoak (Pty) Ltd (522/94) [1996] ZASCA 119; 1997 (1) SA 416 (SCA); [1997] 1 All SA 134 (A); (27 September 1996) About SAFLII Databases Search Terms of Use RSS Feeds South Africa: Supreme Court of Appeal You are here: SAFLII >> Databases >> South Africa: Supreme Court of Appeal >> 1996 >> [1996] ZASCA 119 | Noteup | LawCite Premier Hangers CC v Polyoak (Pty) Ltd (522/94) [1996] ZASCA 119; 1997 (1) SA 416 (SCA); [1997] 1 All SA 134 (A); (27 September 1996) Download original files PDF format RTF format CO CASE NUMBER: 522/94 IN THE SUPREME COURT OF SOUTH AFRICA (APPELLATE DIVISION) In the matter between: PREMIER HANGERS CC Appellant and POLYOAK (PTY) LTD Respondent CORAM: HEFER,KUMLEBEN, HOWIE, ZULMAN et PLEWMAN JJA HEARD ON: 16 AUGUST 1996 DELIVERED ON: 27 SEPTEMBER 1996 JUDGMENT PLEWMAN JA 2 Both the appellant, Premier Hangers Close Corporation ("Premier"), and the respondent, Polyoak (Proprietary) Limited ("Polyoak"), are manufacturers and suppliers of hangers to the clothing industry. Each may also engage in other activities but it is in the clothing field that they compete. The litigation between them, when it commenced, took the form of claims by Polyoak that Premier was passing off hangers manufactured by it as being those of Polyoak. It was alleged but only in the alternative that Premier was guilty of unlawful competition. The proceedings in the Court a quo, also involved claims brought (in the same action) by the joint managing director of Polyoak, Mr H J Louw ("Louw"), alleging infringement by Premier of certain designs, registered in terms of the Designs Act 57 of 1967 ("the Act"), of which he is the proprietor. This part of the litigation was disposed of by a consent order - Premier in effect conceding the claim. The Court 3 a quo held against Polyoak on its claims based on passing off. There is no cross-appeal against this order. It however held against Premier on the claim based on unlawful competition and what is in issue in the appeal is the Court's order on that claim. It is perhap
perturbation.justification: The original text uses 'shall protect...using the same standard of care', creating a clear obligation tied to the Receiving Party's existing practices. The changed text uses 'shall endeavor to protect...using a reasonable level of care' and 'generally secure manner', which introduces ambiguity and weakens the defined standard of care. This introduces ambiguity making it difficult to enforce under state or national law. The Perturbation explanation highlights this shift to a 'vague and open to interpretation' standard, which can cause non-compliance with regulatory requirements, which means legal obligations are open to dispute, per the definition provided. Scraped law snippet 2 mentions a case about unlawful competition, indicating the importance of fair practices. While it doesn't directly state the standard of care, it implies that unclear terms can lead to legal disputes, reinforcing the contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 7.3 The Receiving Party shall comply with a request in terms of this clause, within 14 (fourteen) calendar days of receipt of such a request.
perturbation.changed_text: 7.3 The Receiving Party shall attempt to comply with a request in terms of this clause, within approximately 14 days of receipt of such a request.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'shall comply' with 'shall attempt to comply' and replacing '14 (fourteen) calendar days' with 'approximately 14 days' introduces ambiguity and weakens the obligation. It's unclear what constitutes an 'attempt' or what deviation from 14 days is acceptable, potentially conflicting with contractual certainty requirements under South African law.
perturbation.law_citation: General Principles of Contract Law (South Africa)
perturbation.law_explanation: South African contract law requires certainty in contractual terms. Vague phrases like 'attempt to comply' and 'approximately 14 days' create uncertainty, making it difficult to determine if a breach has occurred and undermining the enforceability of the clause. Parties have to act in good faith, something that may not be measurable.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Contract law legal definition of contract law Contract law legal definition of contract law https://legal-dictionary.thefreedictionary.com/contract+law Printer Friendly contract (redirected from contract law ) Also found in: Dictionary , Thesaurus , Medical , Financial , Encyclopedia , Wikipedia . contract 1) n. an agreement with specific terms between two or more persons or entities in which there is a promise to do something in return for a valuable benefit known as consideration. Since the law of contracts is at the heart of most business dealings, it is one of the three or four most significant areas of legal concern and can involve variations on circumstances and complexities. The existence of a contract requires finding the following factual elements: a) an offer; b) an acceptance of that offer which results in a meeting of the minds; c) a promise to perform; d) a valuable consideration (which can be a promise or payment in some form); e) a time or event when performance must be made (meet commitments); f) terms and conditions for performance, including fulfilling promises; g) performance. A unilateral contract is one in which there is a promise to pay or give other consideration in return for actual performance. (I will pay you $500 to fix my car by Thursday; the performance is fixing the car by that date). A bilateral contract is one in which a promise is exchanged for a promise. (I promise to fix your car by Thursday and you promise to pay $500 on Thursday). Contracts can be either written or oral, but oral contracts are more difficult to prove and in most jurisdictions the time to sue on the contract is shorter (such as two years for oral compared to four years for written). In some cases a contract can consist of several documents, such as a series of letters, orders, offers and counteroffers. There are a variety of types of contracts: "conditional" on an event occurring; "joint and several," in which several parties make a joint promise to perform, but each is responsible; "implied," in which the courts will determine there is a contract based on the circumstances. Parties can contract to supply all another's requirements, buy all the products made, or enter into an option to renew a contract. The variations are almost limitless. Contracts for illegal purposes are not enforceable at law. 2) v. to enter into an agreement. (See: consideration , contract of adhesion , unilateral contract , bilateral contract , oral contract ) Copyright © 1981-2005 by Gerald N. Hill and Kathleen T. Hill. All Right reserved. contract the branch of the law of obligations that deals with obligations voluntarily assumed. Civil law jurisdictions share the inheritance of Roman law, but the canon law idea of pacta sunt servanda (‘promises ought to be obeyed’) has had a considerable influence. The main development since classical Roman law has been the movement away from having a law of mainly specific contracts like hire, service or sale and accepting that consent underlies them all. In the Anglo-American jurisdictions the need for CONSIDERATION is superimposed. The requirements for a contract in Anglo-American law are that there be an offer, an acceptance, consideration and an intention to effect legal obligations. Scots law, because of its civilian origins, does not require the consideration. Contractual consent is generally discovered by objectively, rather than subjectively, investigating the parties' positions. The possibility that they have not actually reached agreement on the same thing - consensus ad idem- is treated under the law relating to mistake or error. See also UNFAIR CONTRACT TERMS . Collins Dictionary of Law © W.J. Stewart, 2006 CONTRACT. This term, in its more extensive sense, includes every description of agreement, or obligation, whereby one party becomes bound to another to pay a sum of money, or to do or omit to do a certain act; or, a contract is an act which contains a perfect obligation. In its more confined sense,
perturbation.justification: The modified text replaces 'shall comply' with 'shall attempt to comply' and changes '14 (fourteen) calendar days' to 'approximately 14 days,' introducing ambiguity. The LLM Law Explanation states that South African contract law requires certainty in contractual terms and that vague phrases create uncertainty, undermining enforceability. The definition of 'Ambiguity Legal Contradiction' states that a legal contradiction happens when an obligation is introduced ambiguously, making it difficult to enforce under state or national law, leading to non-compliance and potential disputes.

perturbation.original_text:
15 NON- SOLICITATION Unless agreed to the contrary between the parties each Party hereby undertakes in favour of the other that it will not at any time during the currency of this Agreement and for a period of 12 months after the expiry or termination of this Agreement (for any reason whatsoever), whether directly or indirectly:
15.1 encourage or entice or incite or persuade any employee of the other (or any employee of any member of each other's group of companies or any entity in which either is interested) to terminate their employment by the other of th em (or any member of its group of companies or any entity in which either is interested); and/or
perturbation.changed_text: 15 NON- SOLICITATION Unless agreed to the contrary between the parties each Party hereby undertakes in favour of the other that it will endeavor not to, at any time during the currency of this Agreement, encourage any employee of the other to consider alternatives to their current employment.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause contains a clear restriction on soliciting employees for 12 months after termination. The modified text significantly weakens this obligation. 'Endeavor not to' is a vague commitment, and 'consider alternatives' is much less restrictive than 'terminate their employment.' This ambiguity could make the non-solicitation clause unenforceable under South African law.
perturbation.law_citation: Magna Alloys and Research (SA) (Pty) Ltd v Ellis 1984 (4) SA 874 (A)
perturbation.law_explanation: South African law, as established in Magna Alloys v Ellis, allows for restraints of trade if they are reasonable. The original clause, with its defined scope and duration, is more likely to be considered reasonable. The modified text's ambiguity makes it difficult to determine if it's a genuine restraint, potentially rendering it unenforceable. A court reviewing such a clause would struggle to assess its reasonableness due to the lack of clarity. The wording is also too vague to protect any proprietary interest of the business.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: statute of limitations | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you statute of limitations A statute of limitations is any law that bars claims after a certain period of time passes after an injury. The period of time varies depending on the jurisdiction and the type of claim. There are statutes of limitations for both civil and criminal causes of action . They may begin to run from the date of the injury, the date it was discovered, or the date on which it would have been discovered with reasonable efforts. Many statutes of limitations are actual legislative statutes , while others may come from judicial common law . Compare: s tatute of repose . [Last reviewed in June of 2024 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex THE LEGAL PROCESS courts statutory interpretation wex definitions courts and procedure criminal law and procedure legal education and practice statutes Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The original clause provides a clear, enforceable non-solicitation agreement with a defined duration (12 months). The modified text introduces ambiguity by using the phrase 'endeavor not to,' which weakens the obligation. This ambiguity makes the clause difficult to enforce, as stated in the Law Explanation. The Perturbation Explanation indicates this ambiguity could make the non-solicitation clause unenforceable. The law snippet discusses statute of limitations, but it does highlight the need for claims to be barred after a "certain period of time passes after an injury." This supports that legal obligations need a defined time frame, which the changed text undermines.
